Tafseer:
Depicting the Resurrection and its conditions:
A question asked in this manner is not intended to seek an answer, but to express the terror or grandeur of something. Although the address here can be general, context indicates that the addressee is the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). Consequently, connected to this, He states further on:
(So remind, [O Muhammad]; you are only a reminder) (verse 21).
'Ghashiyah' means that which covers and overwhelms. Here, this word is used as an attribute of the Resurrection because its calamity will be a universal one, covering everyone, with no escape for anyone. This report (hadith) is narrated to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), but the objective—as will be clear from the subsequent verses—is to warn those disbelievers who did not believe in the Hereafter, or who were completely untroubled by it based on the assumption that they would enjoy even greater ease there than what they enjoyed here.

The state of those who were untroubled by the Resurrection:
As noted, the preceding question was not intended to seek an answer, but was a warning to make the listeners pay attention. Following this, the Qur'an answered it: on that Day, many faces will be completely humbled and exhausted.
'Khashi'ah' means humbled, low, and depressed. 'Amilah' means exhausted with labor, and 'nasibah' means weary and worn out. The meaning is: on that Day, when they face the reality—contrary to their expectations—that they are to be cast into Hell for their deeds, their faces will turn pale and dazed.
Although 'wujooh' refers to persons, they are described as 'faces' because the objective is to depict their inner states, which are reflected most clearly through the faces.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 2 and 3 was combined in the original text.]

This describes what will cause the panic and dazed state described above—namely, they will enter a hot Fire and drink from a boiling spring. 'Aniyah' refers to a spring whose heat has reached its peak.
Various places of the Holy Qur'an clarify that this panic and distress will affect the criminals when they realize they are to be cast into Hell. In Surah Al-Qiyamah, He states:
(And faces, that Day, will be distorted, realizing that there will be done to them a back-breaking calamity) (Al-Qiyamah, 75:24-25).
Generally, commentators have assumed that this describes their state after entering Hell, but this is incorrect. After entering Hell, their faces will not merely be depressed, but they will be dragged on their faces in the Fire and face all other punishments of Hell.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 4 and 5 was combined in the original text.]

The food of the people of Hell:
Following the drink, this describes the food they will receive in Hell. It is stated: they will receive nothing to eat except 'dharee'' (a thorny, poisonous plant) and drink boiling water. 'Dharee'' is a thorny, poisonous plant that no animal touches.
The objective here is not restriction—showing that their food will be only 'dharee''. Rather, this is an exceptional clause (istithna'-i-munqati'). Restriction would occur if 'dharee'' was a food in any sense; since it is not food at all, the exception shows that when they receive no food, they will be forced by hunger to consume 'dharee'' which is present there. This does not negate other things that will be present in Hell and which they will be forced to eat. Elsewhere, it is stated that they will eat 'Zaqqum':
(Indeed, the tree of zaqqum is the food of the sinner) (Ad-Dukhan, 44:43-44).
Elsewhere, 'ghisleen' (discharge of wounds) is also mentioned:
(Nor any food except from the discharge of wounds, which none will eat except the sinners) (Al-Haqqah, 69:36-37).
This reveals that the people of Hell will receive nothing to eat, but only things that are not food at all, and which none but the people of Hell can swallow.

'Which neither nourishes nor avails against hunger' (la yusminu wa-la yughnee min-joo') - Food has two primary benefits: maintaining the body's strength and removing hunger. This will do neither. They will experience only the pain of chewing and swallowing it.

The state of the believers:
This describes the second group—the believers. It is stated: on that Day, many faces will be joyful and radiant.
This appeared in Surah Al-Qiyamah as: (Faces, that Day, will be radiant, looking to their Lord) (75:22-23). In contrast to the panic, depression, and weariness of the deniers, this describes the state of those who lived in this world preparing for the Hereafter, and succeeded in their trial. Their faces will be radiant with the joy of eternal victory.

'Pleased with their effort' (li-sa'yiha radiyah) - This joy will appear on their faces because they will see the result of their earnings in the world, and they will be completely satisfied that their every effort was rewarded fully and the promises of their Lord were fulfilled. This is detailed in the subsequent verses, and a specific aspect is explained in the next Surah.

'In an elevated garden' (fi jannatin 'aliyah) - This describes their resting place: they will be in high gardens. An 'elevated garden' (jannatin 'aliyah)—namely, gardens on high grounds—was the finest concept of a garden for the Arabs, appearing beautiful from a distance and remaining safe from floods.

The assembly of Paradise will be safe from all evil:
It is stated in the Qur'an that when the people of Hell enter, they will curse one another: 'So-and-so led us astray; if he had not, we would have been guided.' The followers and their leaders will argue, and the followers will demand double punishment for the leaders, who will respond: 'We only called you, and you chose to follow.' In contrast, the people of Paradise will welcome one another with peace and respect, exchanging joy, sitting on couches facing one another. Their assembly will be filled with love and sincerity, as stated in Surah Al-Waqi'ah:
(They will not hear therein any ill speech or commission of sin, only a saying of 'Peace, peace.') (Al-Waqi'ah, 56:25-26).
Note also that the wine of Paradise will not affect their intellect, causing them to speak incoherently.

The beautiful scenes of Paradise:
Following the pleasant atmosphere, this points to the beautiful scenes: therein is a flowing spring. This refers to the spring that waters the garden, which does not mean there will be only one spring. Indeed, Surah Ad-Dahr mentions multiple springs and streams. There is no contradiction between the two descriptions.

The furnishings of Paradise:
This describes the furnishings and decorations arranged for their comfort. Their details are explained in different words in various Surahs. This difference is mostly in terms of conciseness and detail, but in some places, it reflects the difference in the ranks of the people of Paradise. Keep in mind that these are described as metaphors; the unseen realities can only be described through metaphors and names familiar to the addressees.
'Therein are couches raised high' (feeha sururum-marfoo'ah) - Couches raised high. The rulers of the time sat on elevated thrones; hence, the metaphor uses this. However, Paradise will be in accordance with the desire of everyone; they will find its furnishings exactly as they wish.
'And cups put in place' (wa akwabum-mawdoo'ah) - 'Akwab' is the plural of 'kawb', which means cups or vessels. 'Mawdoo'ah' means arranged with care.
'And cushions lined up' (wa namariqu masfoofah) - 'Namariq' means carpets or rugs. That is: in every sitting place, carpets and rugs will be arranged next to one another, leaving no place empty.

'And carpets spread around' (wa zarabiyyu mabthoothah) - 'Zarabiy' is the plural of 'zurbiyyah', which means cushions and pillows—meaning cushions and pillows will be spread everywhere over the carpets for their comfort.

Reminding of some signs in nature:
This draws the attention of those who were living heedless of the Resurrection to some prominent signs in nature—showing that they should reflect on the signs of His providence, power, wisdom, and majesty, which bear witness that denying the Resurrection is impossible. The meaning is: with these signs present, why do they persist in demanding to see a sign of punishment or the Resurrection before believing.
Pointing to the camel: 'Then do they not look at the camels—how they are created?' (afala yandhuroona ila-l-ibili kayfa khuliqat) - First, they are directed to the camel, which is their most loyal, serving, and sacrificing companion in travel and settlement. They should reflect on the qualities and characteristics His power has endowed in it—showing how He has made this massive and tall animal subservient to them, such that they can lead it wherever they wish by its rein, and it obeys without hesitation. It is their companion in settlement, their transport in travel, and their ship in the desert. It can endure hunger and thirst for a week, feeding on thorny bushes, without refusing any difficult task. Its flesh, skin, milk, and everything are of use to them, and even its waste is not useless. Now, reflect: did this animal with such diverse benefits come into existence on its own, and did humans subserviate it by chance, or did the Lord of Mercy create it with His power and wisdom for human benefit? Intellect bears witness to the second. If so, is it not obligatory on man to live as a grateful servant of his Lord Who provided these blessings without any right? Otherwise, let him be prepared to face the account of his ungratefulness before his Lord.
Note that the camel is mentioned as an example due to these unique characteristics, representing all the animals His power has subserviated for man, and upon which his life depends. The Qur'an has referred to them in various places to clarify: every blessing requires gratitude, which requires a Day to reward the grateful and punish the ungrateful. This will be discussed in detail in Surah Al-Adiyat.

Pointing to the heaven:
'And at the sky—how it is raised?' (wa ila-s-sama'u kayfa rufi'at) - Since the objective is to direct them to prominent signs, having mentioned the tall camel, He directed them to the heaven: look at how this ceiling is raised! This vast ceiling is raised, yet no one can see any columns supporting it. It has stood for ages, yet the most skilled expert cannot find a single rift or crack in it. Although it is so distant that no one knows its distance, its sun, moon, and stars are the source of light, heat, and life-giving power for the earth, and rain descends from it to sustain life.
Let man reflect: can the Creator Who was powerful over constructing this heaven find it difficult to resurrect him after he has turned to dust! Thus, the Qur'an repeatedly asks: is creating you more difficult, or constructing the heaven?

Pointing to the wonders of the earth:
'And at the mountains—how they are erected?' (wa ila-l-jibali kayfa nusibat) - After showing the wonders of the heaven, He directed them back to the earth, pointing to the greatest sign of His power and wisdom in the earth—the mountains. It is stated: look at how the mountains are erected. They preserve the earth's balance, preventing it from swaying. They control the winds and clouds to apportion rain in accordance with His wisdom. Although they are solid rocks, His power has opened springs of sweet water from them to sustain life. They are the guardians of His precious treasures, which man discovers and uses to develop his civilization. Some mountains are impassable, but He established passes and paths in them so that they do not block communication between nations. Let man reflect: do they not bear witness to His great power, wisdom, and universal providence! And let him reflect: will the Lord Who possesses these attributes leave man unbridled, without bringing a Day to take his account and reward or punish him? Is this not the requirement of His providence and wisdom? Can anyone claim that this is outside His power and impossible!!

From the mountains to the level earth:
'And at the earth—how it is spread?' (wa ila-l-ardi kayfa sutihat) - Now, He directed them from the mountains to the level earth: look at how it is spread beneath your feet. How His providence has created everything necessary for your life in every corner. You build your homes on its level lands, and cultivate your fields and gardens in its plains. Its rivers, wells, and springs water your fields, and its forests and valleys provide pasture for your cattle. Look at these things and reflect: does He Who settled you in this prepared home—where you are using all its resources—have no concern with who lives in accordance with His pleasure and who behaves arrogantly? Intellect says that He has concern, and must have. If He did not, it would mean He is devoid of justice and wisdom, or is helpless. But the One Whose power, wisdom, providence, and majesty you have witnessed cannot be assumed to be indifferent or helpless; so you must accept that He is observing what you do in this home. One Day, this trial will end, and He will gather everyone to judge them. Those who pleased Him will receive His mercy, and those who caused corruption will face His punishment.
'How they are created' and 'how it is raised' are stated briefly; their details have appeared in other Surahs, and the explanation provided here is based on them. If anyone wishes to understand all aspects, they should refer to those places of the Qur'an.
Coherence of the discourse: There is a specific coherence here, combining both ascending and descending orders. The objective is to direct them to prominent signs so that they have no escape. Thus, first He pointed to the camel, whose benefits were undeniable to them. Then, He directed them to the sky above. Then, returning to the earth, the mountains came in the middle, and He pointed to them, followed by the level earth.
Two of these signs—the camel and the earth—are prominent regarding providence, and two—the sky and the mountains—are prominent regarding His power and wisdom. On these very attributes rests the entire philosophy of the Resurrection, معاد (return), and recompense.

Addressing the Prophet:
Having explained the proofs of the warning, this addresses the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to comfort him: if they are denying your warning, it is not because there is any lack of proofs. Proofs are present in every corner of the heavens and earth, but only those who possess fear of God benefit from them, as stated in the previous Surah: (He who fears will remember) (87:10). As for those whose hearts are hardened, they do not benefit from these signs, as stated in the previous Surah: (But the wretched one will avoid it) (87:11). The meaning is: continue your warning and communication, secure in the knowledge that your duty is only to remind.
It is not your responsibility to force them to believe. Allah sent you to remind, not as a guardian over them to be questioned for their disbelief.

Before the exceptive clause, a portion of the discourse is omitted based on context: 'Those who are worthy of guidance will benefit from your call', but as for those who turn away and disbelieve—Allah will punish them with the greatest punishment.

'The greatest punishment' ('adhab-al-akbar) refers to the punishment of Hell, which will be far greater than any worldly punishment. No worldly punishment can match its intensity or permanence. In the previous Surah, this was described as 'the Great Fire' (an-nar-al-kubra).

That is: no one should remain under the delusion that this is merely a threat; it is a certain reality. Every soul must return to Us, and none besides Us, and We must take their account and recompense them. If We did not do so, it would mean this world is a plaything and a vain work, whereas He is Wise, and His works are pure from being vain.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 25 and 26 was combined in the original text.]
